results.title: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
results.content:
Memory for a long-horizon LLM agent is a contract about what each future decision is allowed to see. The simplest contract appends past observations, tool calls, and reflections to every prompt, which makes prior context easy to access but also turns it into a jumbled mixture in which the effect of any single memory component is hard to isolate. We introduce and instrument an alternative bounded contract: every decision is made from a fresh user message assembled by typed retrieval, with no raw cross-decision transcript appended. The prompt thus stays bounded across runs of any length, and any single layer can be ablated in isolation. We instantiate the contract in Slay the Spire 2, a closed-rule stochastic deck-building game whose runs require hundreds of tactical and strategic [...] tests of the contract variable itself. We release a reproducible testbed: 298 completed trajectories with condition tags, frozen memory/skill snapshots, prompt records, and analysis scripts -- an agent design and a validated, reusable methodology for studying how explicit memory layers shape long-horizon LLM-agent decisions. [...] Hugging Face's logo

# AgenticSTS: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents

ShandaAI

## Abstract

A bounded contract approach for long-horizon LLM agents uses typed retrieval to assemble fresh prompts, enabling isolated analysis of memory components and demonstrating improved performance in complex decision-making tasks.

results.title: AgenticSTS Testbed Doubles LLM Wins in Slay the Spire 2 | AI Weekly
results.content:
Read the original article → 

Original headline: HF Paper 'AgenticSTS': Bounded-Memory Testbed Uses Slay the Spire 2 to Study Long-Horizon LLM Agents — Adding Strategic Skills Layer Doubles Wins From 3/10 to 6/10 Where Frontier LLMs Previously Reported Zero

Free AI alerts in your inbox Breaking AI news 3x/week. 44,000+ subscribers.

We use essential cookies to keep the site working (login, form security). With your permission, we also use analytics cookies to understand how you use the site. Privacy policy [...] Most agent research right now is a race to stuff more tokens into context and hope the model sorts it out. The AgenticSTS paper on Hugging Face goes the other way. Every decision is made from a fresh user message assembled by typed retrieval, no raw cross-decision transcript is appended, and prompts stay bounded across runs of any length. The point of the design is that individual memory layers can be ablated in isolation, which is the thing that has been missing from most long-horizon agent write-ups.

results.title: Supersede: Diagnosing and Training the Memory-Update Gap in LLM Agents
results.content:
Table 1 and Figure 2 report the result. For
gpt-4.1-mini, accuracy falls from 82%82\% to 63%63\%: the paired test counts 1919
questions full-context answers correctly but bounded-memory does not, against
only 44 in reverse (p=0.0035p=0.0035). On the frontier, gpt-5.4 raises full-context
accuracy to 92%92\% but bounded-memory only to 77%77\% (p=0.0033p=0.0033; 1313 vs. 11). Across the family, bounded-memory accuracy (63,64,7763,64,77) climbs far more
slowly than full-context accuracy (82,91,9282,91,92), which saturates. The
bottleneck is memory maintenance, not comprehension, and scaling the model only
partially relieves it. (Roughly 13%13\% of questions are wrong even in full
context, confirming real updates are genuinely hard.)

#### Failure modes. [...] #### Conditions.

Full-context places every session in the model
context and asks the question (an upper bound where memory is not the
bottleneck). Bounded-memory is the Supersede agent of
Section 3: a notes field of BB characters (B=300B{=}300 unless noted),
rewritten one session at
a time, with raw sessions never re-fed. For the scale study we vary both the
history length (oracle vs. \_​s\\_s) and the budget BB, including a
constant-ratio setting in which BB grows proportionally with the number
of sessions (150150 characters per session, matching the oracle ratio).

#### Models and significance. [...] The diagnosis above uses proprietary frontier
models, which we cannot fine-tune; to test whether the environment’s reward is
a usable learning signal we therefore switch to an open, trainable
model, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (Qwen Team, 2025). We train with GRPO
(Shao et al., 2024), which estimates its baseline from a group of
sampled rollouts rather than a learned value critic (a natural fit for our
programmatic, verifiable reward), on the prime-rl stack (LoRA, rank
3232, α= 64\alpha\,{=}\,64 (Hu et al., 2022); learning rate 10−510^{-5}; batch 3232,
group 88; 4×4\times A10G GPUs). Training tasks come from the procedural generator in
bounded-memory mode (a fixed set of 20002000 episodes, disjoint from the real

results.title: Why Your AI Agent Keeps Failing: The Memory Problem No One ...
results.content:
I spent a frustrated weekend thinking the agent’s reasoning was broken. It wasn’t. The reasoning was fine. The problem was that my agent had no real memory architecture just a conversation history getting longer and longer, with no structure, no prioritization, and no way to distinguish “important fact from step 2” from “irrelevant tool…
